sentences.base_language: Khalid was sitting in the café when he saw his old friend Omar.
sentences.target_language: كان خالد جالساً في المقهى عندما رأى صديقه القديم عمر.
sentences.romanized: kan Khalid jalisan fi al-maqha indama ra'a sadiqahu al-qadim Omar.
sentences.literal: Was Khalid sitting in the-café when he-saw his-friend the-old Omar.
sentences.grammarNote: كَانَ خَالِد جَالِساً (kāna Khālid jālisan, Khalid was sitting) — كَانَ (was) + active participle جَالِس (sitting, فَاعِل of ج-ل-س). This structure (كَانَ + participle) describes an ongoing past action. جَالِساً is in the accusative because it is the predicate of كَانَ. عِندَمَا (ʿindamā, when) introduces a temporal clause; رَأَى (raʾā, he saw) is a defective verb from ر-أ-ي.

sentences.base_language: Omar! It's been a long time!
sentences.grammarNote: لقد (laqad) is a particle that introduces a completed action and emphasizes it. It is always followed by a past-tense verb: لقد ذهب (he has gone), لقد مضى (time has passed). It adds a sense of 'indeed' or 'already.'

sentences.base_language: They hugged and sat down together at the table.
sentences.grammarNote: تَعَانَقَا (taʿānaqā, they two embraced) — Form VI dual past of ع-ن-ق (ʿ-n-q, neck/to embrace): تَعَانَقَ = to embrace each other (Form VI implies mutual action). The dual suffix ـَا (-ā) marks exactly two subjects. جَلَسَا (jalasā, they two sat) — dual past of ج-ل-س (j-l-s, to sit). Dual verb forms are a distinctive feature of Classical and Modern Standard Arabic.

sentences.base_language: How have you been? Tell me everything.
sentences.grammarNote: كيف كنت؟ (kayfa kunta) uses كان in the past tense to ask 'how were you?' — not 'how are you?' Arabic often uses past-tense كان for states that continued up to now. The present-tense equivalent would be كيف أنت؟ (kayfa anta).

sentences.base_language: I have been working in another city for two years.
sentences.target_language: كنت أعمل في مدينة أخرى لمدة سنتين.
sentences.romanized: kuntu a'mal fi madina ukhra li-mudat sanatayn.
sentences.literal: I-was I-work in city other for-duration two-years.
sentences.grammarNote: كُنتُ أَعمَل (kuntu aʿmal, I was working) — كَانَ + present verb = past continuous: 'I was working/used to work.' This construction expresses ongoing or habitual past action. سَنَتَين (sanatayn, two years) — dual of سَنَة (year): the dual suffix ـَتَين (-atayn) replaces the ة. لِمُدَّة (li-muddat, for the duration of) — a standard expression for duration.

sentences.base_language: Do you remember the time we studied together for the big exam?
sentences.target_language: هل تتذكر عندما درسنا معاً للامتحان الكبير؟
sentences.romanized: hal tatadakkar indama darasna ma'an lil-imtihan al-kabir?
sentences.literal: Q you-remember when we-studied together for-the-exam the-big?
sentences.grammarNote: تَتَذَكَّر (tatadakkar, you remember) — Form V of ذ-ك-ر (dh-k-r, to mention/remember): تَذَكَّرَ = to remember/recall (reflexive Form V). دَرَسنَا (darasnā, we studied) — past tense Form I of د-ر-س (d-r-s, to study): the suffix نَا = we. الاِمتِحَان (al-imtiḥān, the exam) — Form VIII verbal noun of م-ح-ن (m-ḥ-n, to test): اِمتَحَنَ = to examine, اِمتِحَان = examination.

sentences.base_language: Of course! We stayed up all night at your house.
sentences.target_language: بالطبع! سهرنا طوال الليل في بيتك.
sentences.romanized: bit-tab'! saharna tawala al-layl fi baytak.
sentences.literal: By-the-nature! We-stayed-up throughout the-night in your-house.
sentences.grammarNote: بيتك (baytak) = 'your house.' The suffix كَ (ka) is a masculine singular pronoun suffix meaning 'your.' Arabic attaches pronouns directly to nouns: بيت (house) + كَ (your) = بيتك. Feminine 'your' is كِ (ki): بيتكِ.

sentences.base_language: Those were the best days of our lives.
sentences.grammarNote: أَجمَل (ajmal, most beautiful) — superlative/comparative on أَفعَل pattern from root ج-م-ل (j-m-l, beauty): جَمِيل (beautiful) → أَجمَل (more/most beautiful). أَيَّام حَيَاتِنَا (ayyām ḥayātinā, the days of our lives) — a double iḍāfa: أَيَّام (days, broken plural of يَوم, pattern أَفعَال) + حَيَاتِنَا (our lives: حَيَاة + نَا).

sentences.base_language: Things have changed a lot since then.
sentences.target_language: تغيرت الأمور كثيراً منذ ذلك الحين.
sentences.romanized: taghayyarat al-umur kathiran mundhu dhalika al-hin.
sentences.literal: Changed the-matters much since that the-time.
sentences.grammarNote: كثيراً (kathiran) = 'a lot / much,' used as an adverb here modifying the verb. It is the indefinite accusative form of كثير (kathir). Arabic adverbs of degree are often nouns in the accusative case (with tanwin -an): جيداً (well), كثيراً (a lot), جداً (very).

sentences.target_language: لكن الصداقة الحقيقية لا تتغير أبداً.
sentences.romanized: lakin as-sadaqa al-haqiqiyya la tattaghayyar abadan.
sentences.literal: But the-friendship the-real not changes ever.
sentences.grammarNote: لَا تَتَغَيَّر أَبَداً (lā tattaghayyar abadan, never changes) — لَا + present verb = negated present/future. أَبَداً (abadan, never/ever) reinforces the negation: لَا ... أَبَداً = never. تَتَغَيَّر — Form V of غ-ي-ر (gh-y-r, to change): تَغَيَّرَ = to change (intransitive/reflexive). الصَّدَاقَة الحَقِيقِيَّة — definite noun + nisba adjective from حَقِيقَة (truth/reality).

sentences.base_language: They ordered two coffees and talked for hours.
sentences.grammarNote: قَهوَتَين (qahwatayn, two coffees) — dual of قَهوَة (coffee): the ة becomes ت + dual suffix ـَين. تَحَدَّثَا (taḥaddathā, they two talked) — Form V dual past of ح-د-ث (ḥ-d-th, to narrate/speak): تَحَدَّثَ = to speak/converse. لِسَاعَات (li-sāʿāt, for hours) — لِـ + sound feminine plural سَاعَات (hours, from سَاعَة, hour): an expression of duration.

sentences.base_language: Before leaving, they agreed to meet again soon.
sentences.target_language: قبل المغادرة، اتفقا على اللقاء مجدداً قريباً.
sentences.romanized: qabla al-mughadara, ittafaqa 'ala al-liqa' mujaddadan qariban.
sentences.literal: Before the-departure, they-two-agreed on the-meeting again soon.
sentences.grammarNote: اِتَّفَقَا (ittafaqā, they two agreed) — Form VIII dual past of و-ف-ق (w-f-q, to be suitable/agree): اِتَّفَقَ = to agree/come to an agreement. اِتَّفَقَ عَلى = to agree on. المُغَادَرَة (al-mughādara, the departure) — Form III verbal noun of غ-د-ر: غَادَرَ = to leave/depart. مُجَدَّداً (mujaddadan, again/anew) — Form II passive participle of ج-د-د (to renew): literally 'renewed/refreshed.'
